messages.subject: [R-pkg-devel]  Assumed-size arrays in fortran and memory sanitizer
messages.subject_clean: [R-pkg-devel]  Assumed-size arrays in fortran and memory sanitizer
messages.body_plain:
Hi all,

Thank you all so much for your useful answers!


I managed to solve the issue, by replacing the calls to xDCOPY that led to the overlapping memory by two new subroutines, XDMOVE (for vectors) and XDMOVE2D (for matrices).
(The explanation below is a bit technical.)


*The problem was:*

The original xDCOPY is defined as:
      SUBROUTINE XDCOPY(N, DX,INCX, DY,INCY)

where DX is the vector to copy *from* and DY is the vector to copy *to*, and INCX and INCY are the increments for DX and DY respectively in the DO loop.

An error could be triggered if xDCOPY was used, for instance, as:

           CALL xDCOPY (N, X(J+1), 1, X(J), 1)

as it was called with two overlapping subsections from the same vector (X)

*The solution*

For such cases, a new subroutine, called xDMOVE is now used,
that is defined as:

        SUBROUTINE XDMOVE(N, DX, Ifrom, INCfrom,   Ito, INCto)

where vector DX is passed only once, and
*Ifrom* and *Ito* are the *index* of the first position to copy *from* and to parts of DX.


For instance, the original call:
           CALL xDCOPY (N, X(J+1), 1, X(J), 1)
becomes:
           CALL xDMOVE (N, X, J+1,1,    J,1)

and:
          CALL xDCOPY(N, X,   0, X,  1)
becomes
          CALL xDMOVE(N, X, 1,0,   1,1)




When the input array is a *matrix*, xDCOPY is replaced by xDMOVE2D that is defined as follows:

        SUBROUTINE XDMOVE2D(N, MDX, DX, Ifrom,Jfrom,INCfrom, Ito,Jto,INCto)

where MDX is the leading dimension of the assumed-size matrix DX(MDX,*),
and Ifrom,Jfrom and Ito,Jto are the indices to the first position to copy from and to in DX(I,J).


In practice, XDMOVE2D calls XDMOVE, with the indices to the vector that corresponds to DX:

      ...
      IXvec = (Jfrom - 1)*MDX + Ifrom
      IYvec = (Jto       - 1)*MDX + Ito

      CALL XDMOVE(N, DX, IXvec,INCfrom, IYvec,INCto)



As an example of a call, the original call :
          CALL xDCOPY   (M-ME,    W(ME+1,J), 1, W(KRANKE+1,J), 1)
becomes:
          CALL xDMOVE2D (M-ME, MDW, W, ME+1,J,1, KRANKE+1,J,1)

where W is an assumed-size matrix defined as W(MDW, *)



So, it was not as difficult to fix as I originally feared!
Thanks again for all the valuable suggestions.


karline


	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]

messages.body_plain:
Just submitted a package to CRAN; passed Debian incoming checks, 
failed on Windows for a cryptic reason.  I know `testthat` tests are 
often harder to diagnose from CRAN check runs, but ...

    The *current* CRAN version of the package is failing checks on 
Windows-oldrel and the error log gives me useful information about where 
and why the test failed; this makes me think I should get the same level 
of info from a failed incoming check.

https://www.r-project.org/nosvn/R.check/r-oldrel-windows-x86_64/lme4-00check.html

   In contrast, the check log for the CRAN test, and the corresponding 
test log, don't tell me anything useful (complete contents of the test 
failure log are attached below).

https://win-builder.r-project.org/incoming_pretest/lme4_2.0-2_20260703_223201/Windows/examples_and_tests/tests/AAAtest-all.Rout.fail

   (I'm aware that there's a second, vignette-building error in the 
check log; I'm working on fixing that one.)

https://win-builder.r-project.org/incoming_pretest/lme4_2.0-2_20260703_223201/Windows/00check.log

   Any suggestions/diagnoses?

   (I will also try it on win-builder; at the moment either win-builder 
is working on a big package or the queue is stuck -- nothing has left 
the queue for about 2 hours.)

   cheers
    Ben Bolker

messages.body_plain:
On Sat, 4 Jul 2026 17:04:12 -0400
Ben Bolker <bbolker at gmail.com> wrote:

> These platform reports look identical to me, so I'm having a hard
> time figuring out what could be making the difference ... ??

The library paths must be different. Win-Builder installs the packages
it tests (making it possible to test packages with dependencies not on
CRAN/BioC), but that shouldn't normally cause problems during incoming
CRAN checks like you're currently having. This looks like a crash some
time after the "glmer works for Gamma with small shape parameter" test.
There's no obvious reason for a crash or resource starvation.

A package that was checked soon after lme4 failed to install due to
'rlang' failing to load:
https://win-builder.r-project.org/incoming_pretest/rotations_1.6.7_20260705_010208/Windows/00install.out
Maybe this was Uwe reinstalling rlang to fix the problem?

Otherwise you might have to switch to a more verbose reporter for your
next CRAN submission in order to locate the place of the crash more
precisely:

LogReporter <- R6::R6Class('LogReporter',
 inherit = testthat::Reporter,
 public = list(
  file = '', failures = list(),
  initialize = function(...)
   super$initialize(...),
  start_file = function(filename)
   self$cat_tight("File: ", self$file <- filename, "\n"),
  start_context = function(context)
   self$cat_tight(" Context: ", context, "\n"),
  start_test = function(context, test)
   self$cat_tight("  Test: ", test),
  add_result = function(context, test, result)
   if (inherits(result, c('expectation_success', 'expectation_skip'))) {
    self$cat_tight("."); flush(stdout())
   } else {
    self$failures <- c(self$failures, list(
     list(self$file, context, test, result)
    ))
   self$cat_tight("\n   ", format(result), "\n")
  },
  end_test = function(context, test)
   self$cat_tight("\n"),
  end_context = function(context)
   self$cat_tight("\n"),
  end_file = function()
   self$cat_tight("\n"),
  end_reporter = function() {
   if (!length(self$failures)) return()
   self$cat_tight("\nFailures:\n")
   for (f in self$failures) self$cat_tight(
    " File: ", f[[1]],
    " Context: ", f[[2]],
    " Test: ", f[[3]],
    " Result: ", format(f[[4]]),
    "\n"
   )
  }
 )
)
test_check(pkg, LogReporter)

(This will require adding Suggests: R6, which is no stricter than
Suggests: testthat. ProgressReporter comes close, but it's not as
convenient when running tests from R CMD check with output redirected
into a file.)

-- 
Best regards,
Ivan

messages.subject: [R-pkg-devel] Agentic Skills
messages.subject_clean: [R-pkg-devel] Agentic Skills
messages.body_plain:
I have been wondering if there has been any thought or discussion about having a standardized way to include agentic skills in an R package that a user could then copy to the appropriate place,  or fi this is even desirable.  One problem I see is that since skills can be in natural language it would be harder to catch malicious content,  but I know I have some skills I have developed for my own use that in my last go-rounds on my packages I thought it would be neat to generalize and make available.

Thanks,

-Roy

 - mirai uses inst/skills:

I've also started tracking repo-level LLM metadata, though I probably
need to take a pass at better organizing this project:

https://github.com/MichaelChirico/r-ci-samples

Mike C

messages.subject: [R-pkg-devel] Questions and advice about restoring user's par()
messages.subject_clean: [R-pkg-devel] Questions and advice about restoring user's par()
messages.body_plain:
Hi everyone,

I have some wondering about the restoration of the user's par() in a package's functions. There are several discussions on this list and elsewhere about the general problem, but I haven't found any advice regarding the following specific point. Very thankful to anyone who can give me insight.

Let's say your package has a function that produces a plot by calling core-R functions like graphics::matplot(). These core-R functions are called with specific parameters, owing to the nature of the plots and of what needs to be emphasized. Here's a silly example:

```
pkgfun <- function(){
   graphics::matplot(x = 1:2, y = 1:2, type = 'l')
}
```

The function above creates a plot on some graphic device and, *while the device is open*, the user's par() is actually modified. For example we have

original par():
$xaxp
[1] 0 1 5

modified par() while graphic device is open:
$xaxp
[1] 1 2 5

This modification occurs simply because we have called matplot(), even if we didn't directly call par(... = ...). Note that the original par is restored once we close the graphics device, say with dev.off() or closing its window.

Here I have a first question:

? Does CRAN mean that the user's par() should be restored *once the graphics device is closed*? or right *when the function has finished executing*, even if the graphics device is still open?

I wasn't unable to understand which is meant, from the documentation.

If the answer is "only once the graphics device is closed", then the function pkgfun() is OK and I don't need to worry further (and you don't need to read further).


Let's now assume that CRAN wants the par() restored immediately after the function finishes executing instead. Then pkgfun() above is *not* acceptable in a package (destined to CRAN). One should save the old par() and restore it with on.exit(), as follows:

```
pkgfun2 <- function(){
   oldpar <- par(no.readonly = TRUE)
   on.exit(par(oldpar))

   graphics::matplot(x = 1:2, y = 1:2, type = 'l')
}
```

This new function version restores the original par() right after its execution.

However, I now see a problem with this. Imagine that the user wants to add some annotation or similar to the plot. Let's say they want to add a vertical red line at x?=?1.2. With the original function, the user could simply achieve this with

```
> pkgfun() # produces the plot
> abline(v = 1.2, col = 'red') # user wants to emphasize this x-value
```

then saving the plot somehow, maybe sandwiching the two commands above between a pdf() and a dev.off().

With the "correct" function pkgfun2(), however, this is not possible. No vertical red line will appear, because the graphics parameters in par() no longer reflect that particular graphics window. How to deal with such a case?

Sorry for the long email, hope it's of interest to others!
Kind regards,
Luca

messages.subject_clean: [R-pkg-devel] Questions and advice about restoring user's par()
messages.body_plain:
Luca,

I think you misunderstand the rule: the purpose is to not modify *your* customisation in par() that overrides *graphical* parameters (like line types, colors etc.), it is not about plot coordinate setup which you clearly are supposed to keep if the purpose of the function is to setup a new plot. Also, yes, you have to restore it at the end of the function.

To take your example (slightly expanded):

> a=par(no.readonly = TRUE)
> graphics::matplot(x = 1:2, y = 1:2, type = 'l', lwd=2, col=2)
> b=par(no.readonly = TRUE)
> all.equal(a,b)
[1] "Component ?usr?: Mean relative difference: 2"
[2] "Component ?xaxp?: Mean relative difference: 2"
[3] "Component ?yaxp?: Mean relative difference: 2"


The key here is that matplot makes sure that parameters it changed (like lwd or col) are then restored before the function exits, so the only remaining changes are in parameters that setup the coordinates, but nothing else.

This also means that your pkgfun doesn't need to mess with par() at all. The whole point of the rule is that, if everyone follows it, then you only need to restore par() if you modified it directly using par() - all other functions should restore things correctly.

In addition, you typically don't want to save everything - you only need to save what you changed, so the typical idiom you should use is more like:

f = function() {
 p = par(family="Times")
 on.exit(par(p))
 graphics::matplot(x = 1:2, y = 1:2, type = 'l')
}


> a=par(no.readonly = TRUE)
> f()
> b=par(no.readonly = TRUE)
> all.equal(a,b)
[1] "Component ?usr?: Mean relative difference: 2"  [2] "Component ?xaxp?: Mean relative difference: 2"
[3] "Component ?yaxp?: Mean relative difference: 2"

Cheers,
Simon

messages.body_plain:
Simon, I agree that the three parameters below are fine to modify, but 
some that are more problematic are the outer margin and multiple plot 
settings, e.g. oma and mfrow.

Changing those will affect later plots, so they should be reset.  But if 
they are reset, it becomes difficult to add to the plots.  Perhaps the 
community docs should discuss this issue?

I think the best approach is to reset them, but return the required 
settings so a later function can temporarily restore them for long 
enough to modify the plot, i.e. the sort of thing persp() does.

Duncan Murdoch

Simon

>>>> Hi everyone,
>>>>
>>>> I have some wondering about the restoration of the user's par() in a package's functions. There are several discussions on this list and elsewhere about the general problem, but I haven't found any advice regarding the following specific point. Very thankful to anyone who can give me insight.
>>>>
>>>> Let's say your package has a function that produces a plot by calling core-R functions like graphics::matplot(). These core-R functions are called with specific parameters, owing to the nature of the plots and of what needs to be emphasized. Here's a silly example:
>>>>
>>>> ```
>>>> pkgfun <- function(){
>>>>   graphics::matplot(x = 1:2, y = 1:2, type = 'l')
>>>> }
>>>> ```
>>>>
>>>> The function above creates a plot on some graphic device and, *while the device is open*, the user's par() is actually modified. For example we have
>>>>
>>>> original par():
>>>> $xaxp
>>>> [1] 0 1 5
>>>>
>>>> modified par() while graphic device is open:
>>>> $xaxp
>>>> [1] 1 2 5
>>>>
>>>> This modification occurs simply because we have called matplot(), even if we didn't directly call par(... = ...). Note that the original par is restored once we close the graphics device, say with dev.off() or closing its window.
>>>>
>>>> Here I have a first question:
>>>>
>>>> ? Does CRAN mean that the user's par() should be restored *once the graphics device is closed*? or right *when the function has finished executing*, even if the graphics device is still open?
>>>>
>>>> I wasn't unable to understand which is meant, from the documentation.
>>>>
>>>> If the answer is "only once the graphics device is closed", then the function pkgfun() is OK and I don't need to worry further (and you don't need to read further).
>>>>
>>>>
>>>> Let's now assume that CRAN wants the par() restored immediately after the function finishes executing instead. Then pkgfun() above is *not* acceptable in a package (destined to CRAN). One should save the old par() and restore it with on.exit(), as follows:
>>>>
>>>> ```
>>>> pkgfun2 <- function(){
>>>>   oldpar <- par(no.readonly = TRUE)
>>>>   on.exit(par(oldpar))
>>>>
>>>>   graphics::matplot(x = 1:2, y = 1:2, type = 'l')
>>>> }
>>>> ```
>>>>
>>>> This new function version restores the original par() right after its execution.
>>>>
>>>> However, I now see a problem with this. Imagine that the user wants to add some annotation or similar to the plot. Let's say they want to add a vertical red line at x = 1.2. With the original function, the user could simply achieve this with
>>>>
>>>> ```
>>>>> pkgfun() # produces the plot
>>>>> abline(v = 1.2, col = 'red') # user wants to emphasize this x-value
>>>> ```
>>>>
>>>> then saving the plot somehow, maybe sandwiching the two commands above between a pdf() and a dev.off().
>>>>
>>>> With the "correct" function pkgfun2(), however, this is not possible. No vertical red line will appear, because the graphics parameters in par() no longer reflect that particular graphics window. How to deal with such a case?
>>>>
>>>> Sorry for the long email, hope it's of interest to others!
>>>> Kind regards,
>>>> Luca
>>>>

>>>> R-package-devel at r-project.org mailing list
>>>> https://stat.ethz.ch/mailman/listinfo/r-package-devel
>>>>

messages.body_plain:
Dear Duncan, Simon, Uwe,

Thank you all very much for the explanations and examples. It's much clearer to me now. The simple rule "you have to restore any parameters you set explicitly" seems indeed the intended meaning of unofficial and official documentation (for instance <https://contributor.r-project.org/cran-cookbook/code_issues.html#change-of-options-graphical-parameters-and-working-directory> and <https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/policies.html>). I just wanted to be sure.

Great advice, Simon, about saving and restoring the parameters I modify with `oldpar <- par(...=...)`. "par()" has always overwhelmed me, as it handles so many interesting and important parameters.

Thank Duncan and Uwe about the idea of returning parameters that need to be set again. I was checking how packages like 'tinyplot' handle this kind of need. Luckily in my case the usual `oldpar <- par(...=...)` immediately followed by `on.exit(par(oldpar))` (with `add=TRUE` if needed) works well.

Cheers!
Luca

On 260709 15:48, Uwe Ligges wrote:
> I think Duncan explained i correctly, reset everything but return a list of parameters that need to be set again if you want to, e.g., add to the plot generated with modified parameters
> 
> Best,
> Uwe
>

>> Duncan,
>>
>> Sorry, maybe I wasn?t explicit enough: you have to restore any parameters you set explicitly, which does include mfrow and similar as well.
>>
>> What I was illustrating with the code was just that they are all restored - those that were not are those that are defined by plot.window which is required by design if the function creates a new plot.
>>
>> Any suggestions for improvements of the community documentation are welcome.
>>
>> Cheers,
>> Simon
>>
>>
>>> On Jul 9, 2026, at 11:51, Duncan Murdoch <murdoch.duncan at gmail.com> wrote:
>>>
>>> ?Simon, I agree that the three parameters below are fine to modify, but some that are more problematic are the outer margin and multiple plot settings, e.g. oma and mfrow.
>>>
>>> Changing those will affect later plots, so they should be reset.? But if they are reset, it becomes difficult to add to the plots.? Perhaps the community docs should discuss this issue?
>>>
>>> I think the best approach is to reset them, but return the required settings so a later function can temporarily restore them for long enough to modify the plot, i.e. the sort of thing persp() does.
>>>
>>> Duncan Murdoch
>>>
>>>
>>>> On 2026-07-09 5:11 a.m., Simon Urbanek wrote:
>>>> Luca,
>>>> I think you misunderstand the rule: the purpose is to not modify *your* customisation in par() that overrides *graphical* parameters (like line types, colors etc.), it is not about plot coordinate setup which you clearly are supposed to keep if the purpose of the function is to setup a new plot. >>>> }

messages.body_plain:
One additional option for allowing the user of your function to add to
a plot, but still properly reset graphical parameters, is to include
an optional argument to your function that can be a function that will
be run inside of your function, before graphical parameters are reset.
One advantage of doing this is that you can have your function run the
user supplied function after the coordinate system is set up, but
before the main data is plotted, this means that the data will be "on
top" of the user additions instead of underneath.  The panel.first
argument to the hist function is an example of this (though it expects
an expression rather than a function to be passed in, the function
option is probably safer).

>> On Jul 9, 2026, at 11:51, Duncan Murdoch <murdoch.duncan at gmail.com> wrote:
>>
>> ?Simon, I agree that the three parameters below are fine to modify, but some that are more problematic are the outer margin and multiple plot settings, e.g. oma and mfrow.
>>
>> Changing those will affect later plots, so they should be reset.  But if they are reset, it becomes difficult to add to the plots.  Perhaps the community docs should discuss this issue?
>>
>> I think the best approach is to reset them, but return the required settings so a later function can temporarily restore them for long enough to modify the plot, i.e. the sort of thing persp() does.
>>
>> Duncan Murdoch
>>
>>
>>> On 2026-07-09 5:11 a.m., Simon Urbanek wrote:
>>> Luca,
>>> I think you misunderstand the rule: the purpose is to not modify *your* customisation in par() that overrides *graphical* parameters (like line types, colors etc.), it is not about plot coordinate setup which you clearly are supposed to keep if the purpose of the function is to setup a new plot. Also, yes, you have to restore it at the end of the function.
>>> To take your example (slightly expanded):
>>>> a=par(no.readonly = TRUE)
>>>> graphics::matplot(x = 1:2, y = 1:2, type = 'l', lwd=2, col=2)
>>>> b=par(no.readonly = TRUE)
>>>> all.equal(a,b)
>>> [1] "Component ?usr?: Mean relative difference: 2"
>>> [2] "Component ?xaxp?: Mean relative difference: 2"
>>> [3] "Component ?yaxp?: Mean relative difference: 2"
>>> The key here is that matplot makes sure that parameters it changed (like lwd or col) are then restored before the function exits, so the only remaining changes are in parameters that setup the coordinates, but nothing else.
>>> This also means that your pkgfun doesn't need to mess with par() at all. The whole point of the rule is that, if everyone follows it, then you only need to restore par() if you modified it directly using par() - all other functions should restore things correctly.
>>> In addition, you typically don't want to save everything - you only need to save what you changed, so the typical idiom you should use is more like:
>>> f = function() {
>>>   p = par(family="Times")
>>>   on.exit(par(p))
>>>   graphics::matplot(x = 1:2, y = 1:2, type = 'l')
>>> }
>>>> a=par(no.readonly = TRUE)
>>>> f()
>>>> b=par(no.readonly = TRUE)
>>>> all.equal(a,b)
>>> [1] "Component ?usr?: Mean relative difference: 2"  [2] "Component ?xaxp?: Mean relative difference: 2"
>>> [3] "Component ?yaxp?: Mean relative difference: 2"
>>> Cheers,
>>> Simon

messages.subject: [R-pkg-devel] what to do if package takes over 10min to build?
messages.subject_clean: [R-pkg-devel] what to do if package takes over 10min to build?
messages.body_plain:
On June 30th, I used devtools:release() to submit to CRAN an update to the "oce" package. It was rejected on the same day because the build time exceeded 10 minutes.  (That time has exceeded 10 minutes for years now, but exceptions have been made to the rule.  It is a big package.)

I emailed CRAN to ask what I ought to do.  Also, I asked on this email group for advice and was told I ought to wait because members of the CRAN team are very busy.

As of today, it has been 2 weeks since I wrote to CRAN, and I've not received a reply.  That's not a real concern for me, as I understand completely how busy the summer can be.

Ideally, I'd like to know by September whether CRAN has permanently rejected the "oce" package so I can tell my students how to install updates from another source.

I wonder, do folks on this list think that 2 weeks is long enough to indicate that perhaps my email (or a reply to it) got lost in an email storm?  Or should I just wait a few more weeks?  I'm happy either way.

Thanks.

Dan E. Kelley
Department of Oceanography
Dalhousie University
Halifax, NS, CanadaB3H 4R2
Dan.Kelley at Dal.CA

They are ignoring you because this is your job to figure out. I recommend using a web search engine to get hints for how to solve such issues. Don't try to use more cpus in parallel (that is not allowed on CRAN machines), rather reduce your testing and examples and only do more thorough testing on your own. Avoid accessing the web during package build time. Read archives of this mailing list. Good luck!

messages.body_plain:
To other commenters: the earlier thread makes it pretty clear that
Dan already knows what the issues are, that they've been grandfathered
by CRAN in the past, and that he wants to know if that status can
continue.

   I asked Claude for a fine-grained report on timing. This is on
Linux, so the total check time is only 471 seconds, and there's no
guarantee of perfect proportionality when moving from Linux to Windows
(i.e., some components could change their proportional contribution
significantly ...) -- but it does suggest that there's really not much
low-hanging fruit here.  If the CRAN check is very close to 10 minutes
it might be worth trying to trim some stuff, but otherwise I would say
you should repeat your query to CRAN.

R CMD check --as-cran on oce_1.8-3
===================================

WHERE THE TIME WENT
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Phase                                              Elapsed    % total
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Installing package (compile C/Fortran,               94s       20.0%
  45 source files, 13 MB installed libs)
checking R code for possible problems                 63s       13.4%
  (codetools static analysis, 63 R files)
Examples, --run-donttest pass                          52s       11.0%
Examples, normal pass                                  46s        9.8%
HTML manual (592 man pages)                            43s        9.1%
PDF manual (Rd -> LaTeX -> PDF, 592 pages)              40s        8.5%
Tests (testthat, 3098 assertions)                       29s        6.2%
CRAN incoming feasibility (network check)               23s        4.9%
Re-building vignette outputs (7 .Rmd vignettes)         18s        3.8%
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Subtotal, top 9 phases                                 408s       86.6%
Everything else (~250 fast structural/                  63s       13.4%
  metadata/Rd-syntax checks)
--------------------------------------------------------------------
TOTAL                                                  471s      100.0%

DIAGNOSIS
---------
No single pathological bottleneck -- time is spread across genuinely
large-package overhead, not one runaway step.

- Compilation (94s) is the single biggest cost, driven by the compiled
  C/Fortran extensions (13 MB of installed libs/).
- Examples run twice (normal + --run-donttest, as --as-cran mandates):
  combined 98s (~21%), more than installation. Individual examples are
  all fast (none over 1.2s per oce-Ex.timings; per-example self-times
  sum to only 27.7s, so the rest is per-run R startup/package-load
  overhead x2).
- Manual generation (PDF+HTML, 83s combined, ~18%) is proportional to
  the 592 .Rd pages -- pure documentation volume, not a specific slow
  page.
- codetools (63s) scales with R source size/complexity; not unusual
  for 63 source files with S4 methods.
- Tests and vignette rebuilding are comparatively cheap (29s and 18s)
  despite 60 test files and 7 vignettes.

Bottom line: this is what a large, compiled, richly-documented package
check looks like -- cost is distributed roughly evenly across install/
examples/manual/codetools, with no outlier worth specifically
optimizing unless trimming compiled-code size or \donttest example
runtime.

messages.body_plain:
My apologies.. I missed the grandfathered status.

That said, this package desperately exhibits the main downside of the OOP deep inheritance design philosophy... tight coupling of a large code base. If a more functional approach with OO composition instead of inheritance had been used then it would be relatively easy to split the package up for different use cases with only shared code package dependencies to couple them. Having been burned by the promises of inheritance early in my career I empathize with Dan's plight but would also point out the sunk-cost fallacy will only get worse with time... eventually you have to re-design or die of excess code linkage.

If the package could be split, the relevant documentation could also be split and users would not be saddled with loading code they won't be using. But splitting an S4 inheritance tree is trouble-prone and this migration will almost certainly break existing user code so it would probably need a switch away from S4 and it won't happen quickly. A new package name would probably be least confusing.

messages.body_plain:
Many thanks to all, for the help.

I wonder if there are some documents that might guide me as to present-day best practices on OO.

The 'oce' package began as S3, but I switched it to S4 because (at that time ... many years back) S4 seemed to be recommended.

At one point I tried making a "child" package that reused oce objects.  I found that the child was nearly as large as the parent, but (if I recall correctly) that was on macOS, and someone on linux did not find that result.  This, along with breaking users' code, made me cautious about pursuing that path with existing 'oce', so the suggestion for a new name makes sensse.

For another project (not on CRAN) I tried the S7 system.

And I've read of reference classes, etc.

My concern, for a package that is well over a decade old, is about switching to a new OO design without being quite certain that it would be an improvement.  What I've written above indicates that I have an interest in OO design, but also that I don't have a clear idea of the best such design.

Any suggestions would be welcome, as I've gotten a bit out of touch with these things, much of my work now being done in Julia.

-- Dan Kelley / Oceanography / Dalhousie University / Canada

On Jul 13, 2026, at 9:05?PM, Jeff Newmiller via R-package-devel <r-package-devel at r-project.org> wrote:

NOTICE: This message was sent from outside Dalhousie. Think before you click.

My apologies.. I missed the grandfathered status.

That said, this package desperately exhibits the main downside of the OOP deep inheritance design philosophy... tight coupling of a large code base. If a more functional approach with OO composition instead of inheritance had been used then it would be relatively easy to split the package up for different use cases with only shared code package dependencies to couple them. Having been burned by the promises of inheritance early in my career I empathize with Dan's plight but would also point out the sunk-cost fallacy will only get worse with time... eventually you have to re-design or die of excess code linkage.

If the package could be split, the relevant documentation could also be split and users would not be saddled with loading code they won't be using. But splitting an S4 inheritance tree is trouble-prone and this migration will almost certainly break existing user code so it would probably need a switch away from S4 and it won't happen quickly. A new package name would probably be least confusing.

On July 13, 2026 2:31:14 PM PDT, Ben Bolker <bbolker at gmail.com<mailto:bbolker at gmail.com>> wrote:
To other commenters: the earlier thread makes it pretty clear that
Dan already knows what the issues are, that they've been grandfathered
by CRAN in the past, and that he wants to know if that status can
continue.

 I asked Claude for a fine-grained report on timing. This is on
Linux, so the total check time is only 471 seconds, and there's no
guarantee of perfect proportionality when moving from Linux to Windows
(i.e., some components could change their proportional contribution
significantly ...) -- but it does suggest that there's really not much
low-hanging fruit here.  If the CRAN check is very close to 10 minutes
it might be worth trying to trim some stuff, but otherwise I would say
you should repeat your query to CRAN.

R CMD check --as-cran on oce_1.8-3
===================================

WHERE THE TIME WENT
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Phase                                              Elapsed    % total
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Installing package (compile C/Fortran,               94s       20.0%
45 source files, 13 MB installed libs)
checking R code for possible problems                 63s       13.4%
(codetools static analysis, 63 R files)
Examples, --run-donttest pass                          52s       11.0%
Examples, normal pass                                  46s        9.8%
HTML manual (592 man pages)                            43s        9.1%
PDF manual (Rd -> LaTeX -> PDF, 592 pages)              40s        8.5%
Tests (testthat, 3098 assertions)                       29s        6.2%
CRAN incoming feasibility (network check)               23s        4.9%
Re-building vignette outputs (7 .Rmd vignettes)         18s        3.8%
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Subtotal, top 9 phases                                 408s       86.6%
Everything else (~250 fast structural/                  63s       13.4%
metadata/Rd-syntax checks)
--------------------------------------------------------------------
TOTAL                                                  471s      100.0%

DIAGNOSIS
---------
No single pathological bottleneck -- time is spread across genuinely
large-package overhead, not one runaway step.

- Compilation (94s) is the single biggest cost, driven by the compiled
C/Fortran extensions (13 MB of installed libs/).
- Examples run twice (normal + --run-donttest, as --as-cran mandates):
combined 98s (~21%), more than installation. Individual examples are
all fast (none over 1.2s per oce-Ex.timings; per-example self-times
sum to only 27.7s, so the rest is per-run R startup/package-load
overhead x2).
- Manual generation (PDF+HTML, 83s combined, ~18%) is proportional to
the 592 .Rd pages -- pure documentation volume, not a specific slow
page.
- codetools (63s) scales with R source size/complexity; not unusual
for 63 source files with S4 methods.
- Tests and vignette rebuilding are comparatively cheap (29s and 18s)
despite 60 test files and 7 vignettes.

Bottom line: this is what a large, compiled, richly-documented package
check looks like -- cost is distributed roughly evenly across install/
examples/manual/codetools, with no outlier worth specifically
optimizing unless trimming compiled-code size or \donttest example
runtime.

On Mon, Jul 13, 2026 at 5:14?PM Llu?s Revilla <lluis.revilla at gmail.com> wrote:

Hi Dan,

There are several ways to check the CRAN submission queue, I usually use
{cransays}, https://r-hub.github.io/cransays/index.html, which doesn't
report {oce} in the queue.

I would recommend submitting again, simplifying or optimizing a bit more
the package for CRAN: even if previous allowances were made it is better
for the reviewers and maintainers if packages comply with CRAN's policies.

Last, note that "CRAN submission will be closed from Aug 5, 2026 to Aug 19,
2026 (CRAN team vacation and maintenance work)".

Cheers,

Llu?s

On June 30th, I used devtools:release() to submit to CRAN an update to the
"oce" package. It was rejected on the same day because the build time
exceeded 10 minutes.  (That time has exceeded 10 minutes for years now, but
exceptions have been made to the rule.  It is a big package.)

I emailed CRAN to ask what I ought to do.  Also, I asked on this email
group for advice and was told I ought to wait because members of the CRAN
team are very busy.

As of today, it has been 2 weeks since I wrote to CRAN, and I've not
received a reply.  That's not a real concern for me, as I understand
completely how busy the summer can be.

Ideally, I'd like to know by September whether CRAN has permanently
rejected the "oce" package so I can tell my students how to install updates
from another source.

I wonder, do folks on this list think that 2 weeks is long enough to
indicate that perhaps my email (or a reply to it) got lost in an email
storm?  Or should I just wait a few more weeks?  I'm happy either way.

Thanks.

Dan E. Kelley
Department of Oceanography
Dalhousie University
Halifax, NS, CanadaB3H 4R2
Dan.Kelley at Dal.CA

Dear Daniel,

About overhauling the package and breaking user code. For those who wonder
why `ggplot2` has a 2 in its name: there used to be a `ggplot` package.
Hadley wanted to completely rewrite how the package works and that would
break the user's code. Therefore he started a new package and had the old
one around for a few years so users had to to transition.

Should you want to overhaul your package, that might be the best option.

Best regards,

messages.body_plain:
Advanced R by Hadley Wickham, "Functional Programming" and "Function Factories".

I have not read it, but "A Philosophy of Software Design" by John Ousterhout also seems to be well-regarded.

You could continue to use S4 and just restructure it, but my suggestion is to go back to S3. The technical features of S4 come at a performance and complexity price that I can't justify. Reference/S6/S7 classes attempt to make objects mutable, which opens up certain "actor" style programming models but IMO those are a bad match for what oce is focused on. S4 also offers complex function signature distinctions, but I prefer passing fewer parameters as objects and delegating the behavioral distinctions you would have separated by signature to methods on the objects passed to methods or used in object construction.

The "build a list and freeze it" strategy seen in lm and its support methods is simple and fits well with avoiding side effects and composing new objects using more general purpose ones rather than inheriting from deep interfaces.

messages.subject: [R-pkg-devel] Rules / etiquette: Fork package, modify and release to CRAN
messages.subject_clean: [R-pkg-devel] Rules / etiquette: Fork package, modify and release to CRAN
messages.body_plain:
Hi all, I'd very much appreciate some advice regarding rules and etiquette when forking a package and releasing a modified version of it to CRAN.

For my work, I need the functionality of two packages that have been archived from CRAN about two years ago. Up to now, working with the old versions has been fine. However, some changes in my IT environment require packages to be re-installed from CRAN. In addition, the packages need to be slightly updated as they became incompatible with upstream changes to the C++ library they depend on (via an R package that provides this library).

The source code of the packages is available on GitHub, but their maintainer hasn't been active there for two years now. Likewise, other associated online activity (blog, StackOverflow) stopped around that time, and his remaining R packages on CRAN haven't been updated. There is no reaction to GitHub issues, emails to the maintainer address don't bounce but are not answered. Other modes of contact are not obvious (e.g., instituational email address, academic affiliation).

My understanding is that the GPL-3 license of the packages allows me to fork, modify, and release them to CRAN under a new name. However, I'd like to do this as politely and diligently as possible, following the etiquette that may be specific to the R community, and observing any CRAN-specific rules. In particular, I don't want to irritate the developer. I'm very grateful to him for freely sharing his work, providing some quite unique functionality that is way beyond my own capabilities.

Aside from keeping the GPL-3 license, listing the original developer in all source files as author and copyright holder, and including him in the DESCRIPTION's Authors at R field with roles 'aut' and 'cph', is there anything else I should do? Many thanks!

Daniel

However, I'd like to do this as politely and diligently as possible, following the etiquette that may be specific to the R community, and observing any CRAN-specific rules. In particular, I don't want to irritate the developer. I'm very grateful to him for freely sharing his work, providing some quite unique functionality that is way beyond my own capabilities.

That all looks correct at a first glance. But have you considered _adopting_
the archived packages and maintaining work therein?  I have done that two to three
times in the last few years (one more of a fork/simplification, two as
adoptions).

else using these two existing packages would not need to change anything.

whatever you want "just at github" without going back to CRAN. r-universe
will build you binaries / run tests for you. That said, CRAN holds a higher
standard and is indeed a proper goal.

Thanks for asking and offering to "kick one our common cans" a little
further down the road. It's a key spirit of "open sauce" and good to see.

Cheers, Dirk

I think I was in a similar situation (not exactly the same though).
The R package minty was a fork from the superseded code of the popular
R package readr.

Although legally speaking it is okay to fork and release open source
code, it would be much better to ask for permission from the origin
authors first before releasing it.
https://github.com/tidyverse/readr/issues/1517

As forking can sometimes be perceived as splitting up the community,
asking for permission shows that it's in good faith.

messages.subject: [R-pkg-devel] Identify multiple occurrences of connectivity faster
messages.subject_clean: [R-pkg-devel] Identify multiple occurrences of connectivity faster
messages.body_plain:
Hi all,

Some time ago one of my packages was archived from CRAN because it
accessed the internet and I had checked the connection in one place
but not in others on the same file.
The approach I had used was to disconnect the internet connection and
check the package without it. But this failed to find when there were
multiple attempts to connect to the internet on the same test/example
and only the first one was found.

Recently, I found some tests in a different package that uses many
suggested packages
The way I've been testing if any suggested package is required is
checking it using .Renviron with

_R_CHECK_DEPENDS_ONLY_="true"
_R_CHECK_FORCE_SUGGESTS_="false"

Similarly to the previous situation, when multiple suggested packages
are used on the same files it requires multiple rounds.

In both cases the solution is to check the package as many times as
needed until it no longer fails. It can take a lot of time to get all
the cases if the package has long examples and/or tests.

Does anyone have suggestions to find all these occurrences in a single
attempt? Is there any faster approach to identify those problems (when
code is already written)?

Many thanks,

Llu?s

On 2026-07-21 5:09 p.m., Llu?s Revilla wrote:
> Hi all,
>

> accessed the internet and I had checked the connection in one place
> but not in others on the same file.
> The approach I had used was to disconnect the internet connection andtemp
> check the package without it. But this failed to find when there were
> multiple attempts to connect to the internet on the same test/example
> and only the first one was found.
> 
> Recently, I found some tests in a different package that uses many
> suggested packages
> The way I've been testing if any suggested package is required is
> checking it using .Renviron with
> 
> _R_CHECK_DEPENDS_ONLY_="true"
> _R_CHECK_FORCE_SUGGESTS_="false"
> 
> Similarly to the previous situation, when multiple suggested packages
> are used on the same files it requires multiple rounds.
> 
> In both cases the solution is to check the package as many times as
> needed until it no longer fails. It can take a lot of time to get all
> the cases if the package has long examples and/or tests.
> 
> Does anyone have suggestions to find all these occurrences in a single
> attempt? Is there any faster approach to identify those problems (when
> code is already written)?
I don't think you can do that, but it's fairly easy to detect all calls 
to a particular function.  So if all your Internet connections were via 
download.file(), then just write a function local to your package of the 
form

    download.file <- function(url, ...) {
      message("Trying to download ", url)
      message("Current calls:")
      print(sys.calls())
    }

Then, as long as your code doesn't use "utils::download.file(...)", 
you'll get a message for every attempted download.  But if you access 
the Internet in multiple ways, you'll need to do this for each different 
way.  (And once you've fixed things, remember to delete those dummy 
functions...)

Duncan Murdoch
